With the nurses, the woman from his insurance said to me, ‘You can keep applying, and I’ll keep denying,’ I was like, Oh? F that. I made it my mission. I was ‘locked in,’ as the kids would say. I crashed out, and then I locked it in. And we got it approved after two appeals,” she wrote.
Gayheart Has Called Her Marriage to Dane a ‘Huge Success’
Gayheart has repeatedly expressed pride in the close friendship she and Dane maintained after their their romantic relationship ended.
Gayheart also said that both she and Dane considered their marriage to have been a “success.”

“It’s just a season. It wasn’t a failure. It was a huge success,” Gayheart told E! News. “We were married for, I mean, we are still married, but together for 15 years, and we had two beautiful kids so I think that’s a successful relationship, and that’s how we look at it.”

title: Patrick Dempsey Pays Tribute to ‘McSteamy,’ His Longtime ‘Grey's Anatomy’ Co-Star Eric Dane
summary: Patrick Dempsey remembered Eric Dane, his former “Grey’s Anatomy” co-star who died at the age of 53 announcing his ALS diagnosis last year.
story: Patrick Dempsey is mourning the loss of his former “Grey’s Anatomy” co-star Eric Dane, who died at the age of 53 on Feb. 19 after announcing his ALS diagnosis in 2025.
“I just woke up this morning and I was very sad to read the news,” Dempsey said Feb. 20 during an appearance on Britain’s “The Chris Evans Breakfast Show.”
Dempsey noted his grief and what Dane's death means for  his family.
“Yeah, it’s hard to put into words. I feel really so sad for his children,” he said, referring to his daughters, Billie and Georgia, whom he shared with his ex Rebecca Gayheart.
Dempsey said he and Dane had recently been in contact, but he knew his condition was worsening.

“I was corresponding with him, we were texting, so I spoke to him about a week ago and some friends of ours went in to see him and he was really starting to lose his ability to speak,” he said. “He was bedridden and it was very hard for him to swallow, so the quality of his life was deteriorating so rapidly.”

On “Grey’s Anatomy,” Dane played Dr. Mark Sloan, nicknamed “McSteamy,” while Dempsey portrayed Dr. Derek Shepherd, known for his part as “McDreamy.” Dempsey debuted on the show in the pilot in 2005, but his character died in Season 11 in 2015, although the character has appeared sporadically since.
Dane first appeared on the ABC drama in Season 2 before leaving the show in Season 8 in 2012, with his character also dying.
In the interview Friday, Dempsey recalled Dane's "great sense of humor" as an actor and co-star.

"He was easy to work with. We got along instantly. You know, first scene was him, you know, in all his glory, coming out of the bathroom with the towel on, looking amazing, making me feel completely out of shape and insignificant," Dempsey said.
But he said there "was never really any competition."

“There was just this wonderful mutual respect," Dempsey said. "He’s wickedly intelligent and I’m always going to remember those moments of fun that we had together and celebrate the joy that he did bring to people’s lives."
Dempsey also praised Dane's advocacy work.

“The real loss is for us who don’t have him anymore," he said. "He did an incredible job at bringing awareness to this horrible disease and those remaining days, which was really brave of him to do. And it just reminds us that we all have to celebrate every day like it’s our last day, because I am at the age now where I am wearing glasses because I can’t see.

“... It’s something we have to remember, certainly in a world with there is just so much crisis and there’s so much tragedy that we really need to be grateful for every moment that we have. Spend time with our families, do things that are better, that are the betterment of other people, to be of service, to be kind, to be loving."

title: Eric Dane Shares a Message for His 2 Daughters in His Last Interview
summary: In his last interview special shared on Netflix, Eric Dane said the four life lessons he learned from ALS, directed at daughters Billie and Georgia.
story: Before his death at the age of 53, Eric Dane shared four life lessons with his daughters, Billie and Georgia, in his final interview.

The actor sat down with Brad Falchuk for a Netflix special titled "Famous Last Words: Eric Dane" and reflected on his life and career during an hour-long conversation. Throughout the interview, Dane discussed the impact his ALS diagnosis had on his life and his worldview.
At the end of the special, Falchuk left the room to allow Dane the opportunity to speak to the camera alone and share a message for his daughters.
"I want to tell you four things that I've learned from this disease and I hope you won't just listen to me. I hope you'll hear me," Dane said.
Dane went on to share the four life lessons he learned from his ALS diagnosis, which he wanted to impart on his daughters.
#1 : 'Live in the Present'
Dane began by encouraging his girls to live "right now in the present."

"It's hard but I learned to do that. For years, I'd wander off mentally, lost in my head for a long time, wallowing and worrying in self pity, shame and doubt and replay decisions, second guess myself. 'I should've done this. I never should've done that," he explained.
The actor then outlined why this life lesson is particularly important.
"The past contains regrets. The future remains unknown. So you have to live now. The present is all you have. Treasure it, cherish every moment," he said.
#2: 'Fall in Love'
Dane also expressed a desire for his daughters to fall in love, in multiple ways.

"Fall in love. Not necessarily with a person, although I do recommend that as well. But fall in love with something. Find your passion, your joy, find the thing that makes you want to get up in the morning," he said.
The father of two then explained that he fell in love "for the first time" when he was around his daughters' ages.
"I fell in love with acting. That love eventually got me through my darkest hours, my darkest days, my darkest years. I still love my work. I still look forward to it," he said.
Dane said his work didn't "define" him, but it still excited him.
"Find something. Find something that excites you. Find your path, your purpose, your dream, then go for it. Really go for it," he said.
#3: 'Find Your People'
While outlining his wishes for his daughters, Dane said that he wanted them to choose their friends wisely.

"Find your people and allow them to find you, and then give yourselves to them. The best of them will give back to you, no judgment, no conditions, no questions asked. I'm so thankful for my very close family and friends," he said.
Dane went on to describe how his friends "stepped up" during his diagnosis.

"I can't do the little things I used to do. I can't drive around town and go to the gym and get coffee. Now I've learned to embrace alternatives. My friends come to me. We eat together, watch a game, listen to music. They don't do anything special. They just show up," he said.
Dane encouraged his daughters to love their friends and "hang onto them."
"They will entertain you, guide you, help you, support you. And some will save you," he said.
#4: 'Fight With Every Ounce of Your Being '
Dane asked his daughters to channel his resilience when they face their own life challenges.
"Fight with every ounce of your being and with dignity. When you face challenges, health or otherwise, fight. Never give up. Fight until your last breath," he said.
Dane said that ALS was "slowly taking" his body but said the disease would "never take my spirit."
"The two of you are different people, but you're both strong and resilient. You inherited resiliency from me. That's my superpower," he said.
“You can face hell with dignity. Fight, girls, and hold your heads high.
Eric Dane's Message to his daughters
Dane added some levity to the heavy moment and joked that he was on his 15th life at this point, because he always persevered.
"When something unexpected hits you — and it will, because that's life — fight and face it with honesty, integrity and grace, even if it feels or seems surmountable," he said.
"I hope I've demonstrated that you can face anything. You can face the end of your days. You can face hell with dignity. Fight, girls, and hold your heads high."

title: Kelly Clarkson Opens Up About End of Her Talk Show, What’s Next with ‘The Voice’
summary: Kelly Clarkson has opened up about why she ended her daytime talk show as she gets ready to be a coach on the upcoming Season 29 of “The Voice.”
story: Kelly Clarkson has opened up about why she ended her daytime talk show as she gets ready to be a coach on the upcoming Season 29 of “The Voice.”
The Grammy-winning singer spoke alongside fellow “Voice” coach John Legend on TODAY Feb. 20 about why she decided now was a good time to bring the show to a close after seven seasons.

“I think everybody probably gets the timing,” she told Carson Daly. “Our family life, the dynamic changed a bit, and it has changed for a minute now. You got kids, we’ve all got kids, and it’s one of those things when you kind of start seeing life as how precious it is, too. It’s like, ‘You know what, I’m really busy.’”

Clarkson is a mom to daughter River Rose, 11, and son Remy, 9, whom she welcomed with her late ex-husband, Brandon Blackstock. He died at 48 in August 2025 after three years of being treated for cancer.

“I was like, ‘I know everybody thinks she’s like quitting,’” Clarkson said about the show. “I’m like, ‘I still have other jobs, so I’m still doing stuff.’ There’s just too much on the plate, so I was like you know what, it’s time to pull back.”
The decision was a difficult one for Clarkson between the success of the show and the close-knit relationship with the staff.

“That’s what was really hard,” she said. “I work with a lot of people here, too. The crew’s been incredible, it wasn’t that the show isn’t doing well. That’s what kind of sucked, everything was going well, and that’s what was really hard.
“That was a really hard thing for me, but an easy decision as a momma.”

Clarkson and Legend also gave a preview of Season 29 of “The Voice” — which features a new wrinkle, in that there will only be three coaches instead of the usual four. Out of her nine previous seasons on the show, Clarkson is a four-time winning coach.
“I have won the most, if you’re counting,” she joked. “It was part of my pitch.”

Her team will face off against teams coached by two other icons of the hit NBC talent show, Legend and Adam Levine, in “The Voice: Battle of Champions.” Clarkson also said on TODAY the show will be bringing back some past champions this season as she makes her return to “The Voice” for the first time since Season 23 in 2023.
"I've been gone for a while, so it worked, scheduling," she said.

As a veteran of “The Voice,” Clarkson still enjoys the Blind Audition segments where the coaches sit in swivel chairs with their backs turned and can only hear the singers performing behind them but can’t look at them.

“I love games in general, and I love just being competitive in general, but I do love not knowing who’s behind you,” she said. “And then you have this idea in your head, and you turn around, and I am 90% of the time wrong, like every time. You keep thinking, we’ve done this so many times, there can’t possibly be someone just incredible, and wow.”
“Every season, America just keeps giving us great singers,” Legend said.
The new season premieres on Feb. 23 at 9 p.m. ET on NBC and will be available to stream on Peacock the next day.
Clarkson’s return to “The Voice” comes less than a month after she announced on Instagram Feb. 2 that “The Kelly Clarkson Show” was coming to an end after seven seasons.

“I have been extremely fortunate to work with such an outstanding group of people at (‘The Kelly Clarkson Show’), both in Los Angeles and New York,” she wrote. “There have been so many amazing moments and shows over these seven seasons. I am forever grateful and honored to have worked alongside the greatest band and crew you could hope for, all the talent and inspiring people who have shared their time and lives with us, all the fans who have supported our show, and to NBC for always being such a supportive and incredible partner.”
She shared that her family was a key part of the decision.
“Stepping away from the daily schedule will allow me to prioritize my kids, which feels necessary and right for this next chapter of our lives,” she wrote.

New episodes of Season 7 of “The Kelly Clarkson Show” will air through fall 2026, according to a press release. Since debuting in 2019, the show has racked up two dozen Daytime Emmy awards and achieved continued virality for Clarkson’s signature “Kellyoke” performances.

title: ‘The Traitors’ Season 4, Episode 10 Recap: Who Was Banished and Who Was Murdered?
summary: Did two Traitors successfully make it to the finale? Or, will the Faithfuls have the upper hand going into the last episode? Read a recap of Episode 10 to find out.
story: Warning: Spoilers from Season 4, Episode 10 of “The Traitors.”
The penultimate episode in Season 4 of “The Traitors” saw original Traitor Rob Rausch struggling for the first time in the game as he tried to manage his paranoia and avoid suspicion.

At the start of episode 10, the “Love Island USA” star and singer Eric Nam, a recent recruit to the turret, plotted who to murder next. The two attempted to map out a plan that would ensure they both make it to the finale, airing Feb. 26, along with the reunion. But with only eight players remaining, it was becoming more and more challenging to throw the Faithfuls off their scent. So, after weeks of keeping a target off his back, Rausch was finally questioned by the Faithfuls.

The Faithfuls were desperate to unmask another Traitor because a crucial Round Table was on the way. As host Alan Cumming explained, it was the last time a banished player would reveal their identity before leaving. So, did two Traitors successfully make it to the finale? Or, will the Faithfuls have the upper hand going into the last episode?
Read a recap of Episode 10 to find out what happened.
The Walls Are Closing in on Rob?

Instead of beginning in the turret, Episode 10 opens with the players who weren’t up for murder arriving for breakfast. “Love Island” star Maura Higgins and Rausch are the first two to enter the breakfast room. In his confessional, Rausch reveals that he has been feeling the weight of all his lying and scheming from the past few weeks. He adds that he feels bad for deceiving Higgins and the other Faithfuls who blindly trust him.

Next, fellow Traitor Nam and the remaining Faithfuls — “Dancing With the Stars” pro Mark Ballas, Olympic figure skaters Tara Lipinski and Johnny Weir and “Survivor” winner Natalie Anderson — all arrive, meaning “Top Chef” host Kristen Kish was murdered.
In a flashback, viewers see Kish learning her fate after turning a creepy jack-in-the-box toy in the dungeon that confirmed she was murdered.

“My time here has come to an end, but I’m at peace,” she says in her final confessional. “I was the most faithful of the Faithfuls. I don’t think many people doubted that and I think the Traitors knew that.”

Then, viewers learn that Rausch and Nam decided to murder Kish even though she threw out the singer’s name last episode because they were convinced they could get the players to focus on Anderson instead.

Cumming appears and announces that tomorrow will be the final day in the castle. Before the mission begins, Lipinski tells Anderson that she is starting to doubt that Rausch is a Faithful. She mentions that “The Real Housewives of Potomac” star Candiace Dillard Bassett voted for Rausch twice before she was outed as a Traitor. Anderson agrees with Lipinski and adds that she thinks she hasn’t been murdered because Rausch has been protecting her in the turret. Meanwhile, Rausch and Nam are carrying out their plan of making Anderson look guilty by mentioning her name to Ballas.
On the way to the mission, Higgins alerts Rausch that Lipinski and Anderson are suspicious of him.
Rob Spins Out at the Mission

Going into the mission, Rausch says he wants to secure the shield to ensure he and Nam can murder anyone they want. At the mission, the players have to strap themselves into a spinning ride and hold on. When the ride stops, they must find bags of gold hidden inside stuffed animals scattered around the fair grounds. Then, they must return to the ride for another spin.

But, Cumming has a twist up his sleeve. He doesn’t tell the players that each round one chair on the ride will be removed, eliminating the last player to return. The first round begins and Rausch finds his bag of gold last. When he learns he has been eliminated, Rausch becomes upset, directs his anger toward Ballas and complains about needing a shield, making some of the players question his behavior.
The rounds continue and the last player remaining is Nam, who earns the shield. The group adds $24,500 to the prize pot.
An Emotional Round Table

While returning from the mission, Anderson continues to suggest that Rausch is a Traitor and mentions that his reactions during the mission felt performative. Lipinski also says Rausch’s demeanor has changed, but she worries about being too vocal at the Round Table since she has been wrong every week.

At the Round Table, Cumming tells the player that this is the last time a banished player will reveal if they are a Traitor or a Faithful. The Round Table begins and Lipinski says she doesn’t want to lead the group in the wrong direction, so she will take a back seat. Rausch calls out Anderson for being distant recently. She counters by pointing out he hasn’t been confiding in her. She also brings up her evidence: Candiace’s two votes against him.

But, Ballas and Higgins say that Anderson could be a Traitor that decided to murder all the gamers to make herself appear vulnerable. When Weir and Lipinski remain mostly quiet, Anderson feels manipulated and says she regrets sharing her Rausch theory.
Prior to the vote, Rausch uses his Dagger, allowing him to vote twice. He votes for the “Survivor” alum and all the other players also vote to banish Anderson, who votes for Lipinski.

While standing on the Circle of Truth, Anderson says, “You know, my whole life I’ve wanted to be so strong and I try really hard to present as tough. And sometimes it comes with a negative of being judged, of being ruthless, cutthroat. But today, a gamer got played, I’m a Faithful. So, good job guys.”
A Scorching Murder

After Anderson leaves, Lipinski begins to cry. “I truthfully don’t want to play this game anymore. I feel like a fool. I have made mistakes. But seriously, I am OK with being murdered tonight,” she tells the group before Ballas comforts her.

Rausch says in his confessional that that was the toughest Round Table so far but he “had to do what he had to do.” He then joins Ballas, who is now questioning why Nam didn’t speak at all during the Round Table. Weir voices the same concern in his confessional and theorizes that Rausch and Nam are the two remaining Traitors.
In the turret, Cumming interrupts Nam and Rausch’s discussion about who to murder. He tells them their last kill will be “a scorcher.”

Cumming says that after the Traitors make their decision, all the players will meet outside. “There, they’ll be forced to stare death in the face. I will reveal which player you have murdered and this will happen in front of everyone,” he explains.
The episode ends with all the players standing in front of the castle, where a giant coffin has been placed inside a circle.
In a teaser for the conclusion to the season, Cumming says in a voiceover, “The final showdown is approaching.”
Who will be the last player or players standing? The answer will be revealed on Thursday, Feb. 26.

title: Eric Dane Dies at 53, Nearly 1 Year After Announcing ALS Diagnosis
summary: Eric Dane has died at 53 after announcing his ALS diagnosis in April 2025, his family confirmed in a statement to TODAY.com.
story: Eric Dane has died at 53, his family confirmed in a statement to TODAY.com.
The actor, known for his work in "Grey's Anatomy" and "Euphoria," announced his ALS diagnosis last year, and died on Feb. 19, 2026.
“With heavy hearts, we share that Eric Dane passed on Thursday afternoon following a courageous battle with ALS," the statement began.

"He spent his final days surrounded by dear friends, his devoted wife, and his two beautiful daughters, Billie and Georgia, who were the center of his world. Throughout his journey with ALS, Eric became a passionate advocate for awareness and research, determined to make a difference for others facing the same fight. He will be deeply missed, and lovingly remembered always. Eric adored his fans and is forever grateful for the outpouring of love and support he’s received. The family has asked for privacy as they navigate this impossible time,” it continued.
ALS is a progressive nervous system disease that affects nerve cells in the brain and spinal cord and causes loss of muscle control, according to the Mayo Clinic.
The disease, which is fatal and has no cure, gradually affects control of the muscles needed to move, speak, eat and breathe.
Dane went public with his ALS diagnosis in a statement to People in April 2025. He also expressed gratitude for his family’s support and said he felt "fortunate" to be able to continue working.
Dane quickly became the public face of the disease, which affects approximately 30,000 people in the United States at any given time, according to the ALS Association.

In September 2025, he set a goal of raising $1 billion toward research for treatments for ALS, and announced on Instagram the he had partnered with “I Am ALS” on its Push for Progress initiative to find a cure for the disease.
Dane and his estranged wife Rebecca Gayheart, who tied the knot in 2004, had remained close friends since their 2018 split, vacationing together with daughters Billie and Georgia.
Once Dane was diagnosed with ALS, Gayheart made it a point to “show up” for the actor, she revealed during a Nov. 17, 2025 appearance on the “Broad Ideas” podcast.

"I am definitely trying to show (our daughters) that we show up for people, no matter what. He is our family. He is your father,” she said. “We show up, and we try to do it with some dignity and some grace and just get through it and that we will get through it the best we can.”
Gayheart also dismissed a petition to divorce she filed in 2018 in March 2025, less than a month before Dane announced his diagnosis.
After going public with his health challenges, Dane went on to star in the Amazon Prime Video crime series “Countdown," which premiered in June 2025.
The same month, he told E! News of his acting career, "I’m going to ride this till the wheels fall off." He added that being on the set of a production was good for his mental health.
"It keeps me sharp. It keeps me moving forward, which is super important right now,” he said.
In November 2025, Dane earned rave reviews for guest starring as a firefighter living with the disease in on an emotional episode of the NBC hospital drama “Brilliant Minds.”

title: 'Love Story': Did John F. Kennedy Jr. Really Break Up With Carolyn Bessette Over an Anonymous Letter?
summary: John F. Kennedy Jr. and Carolyn Bessette break up over an anonymous letter in Episode 4 of Love Story. Here's who wrote the letter.
story: John F. Kennedy Jr. and Carolyn Bessette's connection grows in Episode 4 of "Love Story." But so do the problems that come with dating the most eligible bachelor in the U.S. and a scion of a political dynasty.

In the episode, things are going well between John (played by Paul Anthony Kelly) and Carolyn (Sarah Pidgeon). Sure, Carolyn's mom is suspicious of their rekindled relationship — but maybe this time, it's different.

Then John finds a letter slipped into his bag after a game of soccer casting doubt on Carolyn. After reading it, everything changes.  John is suddenly suspicious of Carolyn. Is she a user like everyone else?

Doubting her character, John abruptly ends things and Carolyn shuts the door on him just as fast. One apology and a difficult conversation later, though, they have exchanged "I love yous" and are back together.
Elizabeth Beller writes about the real events that inspired this segment in her book "Once Upon a Time," the basis of the show.

According to Beller, the rupture the letter caused was actually more dramatic than what the episode shows. John did receive a letter about Carolyn two months into their love affair, and it led to a "messy" breakup, per Beller.
According to the book, Kennedy and Bessette met at a Tribeca restaurant called El Teddy's a week after they had a blissful weekend away.

"John presented Carolyn with a letter. The author who sent it to him, a friend of his, came from the milieu of boarding schools, Ivy League universities and 'old money' families of New York, though he didn't divulge those facts until much later. The letter claimed Carolyn was a user, a partier, that she was out for fame and fortune," Beller wrote.
The letter also said that she "dated guys around town."
Beller wrote that Kennedy "tossed the piece of paper at her, stood, and walked out the door."

The fallout lasted a year. Soon afterward, John tried to win her back, to no avail. "Within a week of the scene at the restaurant, John tried to call Carolyn. And call. And call and call and call," she wrote.
Bessette ended up dating Michael Bergin, the model from the first episode of "Love Story."

A year later, in 1993, the two began dating again under unclear circumstances. "Somehow John convinced Carolyn to try again. And she gave it a shot," Beller wrote. "But Carolyn was guarded about the revival of John, treading carefully."
Beller wrote Kennedy was also dating actress Daryl Hannah and model Julia Baker when they reconnected.
They got engaged in 1995 and married in 1996. They died in a plane crash three years later.
As for who wrote the letter? Beller never identifies the person, but says Carolyn eventually figured out who wrote the letter and she would later "freeze them out."

title: Ricky Martin Visited a Coffee Shop. A Sign Inside Happened to Say He Could ‘Sip for Free’
summary: Ricky Martin visited Sip 305, a coffee shop in Miami, Florida, where there just happened to be a sign that said he could drink free.
story: Livin’ la vida loca — or, in this case, mocha — certainly has its perks.

On Feb. 17, a prospective customer walked into Sip 305, a coffee shop located near Florida International University in Miami. He’d never been there before, but as soon as he walked in, he noticed his name on a sign: “People Who Sip for Free.”

Tickled by the coincidence, he took a selfie video with the sign. But even more shocked than he were the shop workers, who couldn’t believe Ricky Martin was right there in front of them, days after performing in the Super Bowl halftime show with Bad Bunny.
“Aye, qué casualidad,” Martin says in a video filmed inside the cafe. “Besos y abrazos. Look at this.”

The board on the wall lists a few celebrities’ names: Pedro Pascal, Bad Bunny, football quarterbacks Drake Maye and Sam Darnold, and, smack-dab in the middle, Martin. Another row has fellow halftime show performer, Lady Gaga, and the band Green Day, who performed during the Super Bowl’s opening ceremony at Levi’s Stadium in Santa Clara, California.

Martin laughs, then pans the camera to Sip 305 employee and FIU senior Nacosi Rosemond, who is shocked into near-silence. She says “um” a few times while staring at the Latin music superstar, who pans back to himself, giggling.
Commenters had a lot to say about the coincidental celebrity encounter.
“Omg they manifested him,” one wrote.
“Imagine not having a shift at this moment,” another said.
A third added, “I didn’t have a shift when Lana Del Rey came at our store. It’s been over a decade and I still can’t let it go.”

Later, Rosemond commented on the post, “the pure shock on my face is frying me.” She says she was washing her hands in the back of the shop when she heard the bell calling her to the front, only to see him standing there.
People seem to relate to the way she reacted to Mr. “Volveras” himself.
“I too would be that girl in the back: flabbergasted, unable to get words out,” one person commented.

“Honestly, the only thing I could think to say to him was, ‘You know, you’re the first celebrity to ever come in on the board,’” Rosemond tells TODAY.com with a giggle, adding that Martin conversed with customers waiting to be helped.
“He was super sweet, told them all about the Super Bowl halftime show, and they asked what his order was going to be,” she says. “He said, of course, a mango smoothie.’”
The cafe posted a second angle of the interaction, showing Martin taking selfies with fans as onlookers stare at the star.
Manager Tiffany Nguyen says the “Free Sips” board, which lists celebrities’ names, is mostly a joke, but the shop’s staff updates it based on current events.

“It has relevant celebrities and, every couple of months, we change it, and we also put one of our Instagram followers on there so that way an actual customer that comes in gets to see their name on the board and they get to feel like a celebrity,” Nguyen tells TODAY.com. “So yesterday, we actually had one of the celebrities come in, and so that was really cool.”
Martin hasn’t posted the video to his social accounts — yet — but Rosemond says they got it because he AirDropped it to them.
Did he ultimately have to pay for his drink?
“Of course we gave it to him for free!” says Rosemond.

title: Who Is Margot Robbie’s Husband? All About Tom Ackerley
summary: Who is Margot Robbie's husband, Tom Ackerley? Here's everything to know about the producer and actor, who had a small role in a "Harry Potter" film, and their son together.
story: Australian-born Margot Robbie has undoubtedly become one of Hollywood’s biggest stars, from the record-breaking success of “Barbie” to her leading roles in “Wuthering Heights”, “I, Tonya,” “Babylon,” “Amsterdam” and more.

Robbie served as both producer and the lead in “Wuthering Heights,” which premiered in theaters on Feb. 13. She also produced and starred in “Barbie,” which made more than $1.4 billion at the box office and cemented the star as Hollywood royalty.
Prior to the controversial Oscars snub in 2023, Robbie was nominated for two Oscars for her performances: best supporting actress in 2020 for “Bombshell” and best actress in 2018 for “I, Tonya.”
The couple, both 35, formed a production agency, LuckyChap Entertainment, in 2014 and married in 2016. The pair now have a child together, who was born in 2024.

Their company is behind “Barbie,” as well as other successful films including Emerald Fennell’s “Promising Young Woman” and “Saltburn.” The company is also supporting the “Wuthering Heights” adaptation.
Ackerley, a producer and actor, got his start behind the camera as a production runner before working as an assistant director and producer on more than a dozen films and TV shows.
Here’s what to know about Ackerley, including his background, how he and Robbie met, his connection to “Harry Potter,” and more.
Who Is Tom Ackerley?

An actor, producer and assistant director, Tom Ackerley, 35, was born in Surrey, England, but grew up nearby in Guildford, reports Surrey Live. He got an early start in the film industry working as an extra in “Harry Potter and the Prisoner of Azkaban,” according to IMDb.
The “Harry Potter” connection is actually a source of amusement for his now-wife.

During a visit to “The Graham Norton Show” in 2020, Robby laughed, “I was like, ‘Had you told me sooner we would’ve been married very quickly. He was an extra when he was a little kid — he was one of the Slytherin extras when Draco pushes some kids out of the way to see Buckbeak. ... He pushes my husband out of the way.”

After college, Ackerley returned to showbiz, but this time behind the scenes. On his IMDb page, he’s credited as a crowd runner and assistant director on films and TV series like “Tinker Tailor Soldier Spy” (2015) and “Da Vinci’s Demons” (2014).
While working as a third assistant director on 2014’s “Suite Française,” he met Robbie (who had a small role alongside Michelle Williams and Kristin Scott Thomas) and the pair clicked.
What Happened after Tom Ackerley and Margot Robbie Met?
“I was the ultimate single gal,” Robbie told Vogue in 2016.
“The idea of relationships made me want to vomit,” she continued. “And then this crept up on me."
She described Ackerley as “the best-looking guy in London.”

"We were friends for so long. I was always in love with him, but I thought, Oh, he would never love me back. Don’t make it weird, Margot. Don’t be stupid and tell him that you like him. And then it happened, and I was like, Of course we’re together. This makes so much sense, the way nothing has ever made sense before,” she said.
Though they remained private about the details, the pair could be found in public together. They attended a New York Rangers game at Madison Square Garden in New York City in 2015.
What’s LuckyChap Entertainment?

Shortly after they met, the pair became producing partners with fellow “Suite Française” assistant directors Josey McNamara and Robbie’s childhood pal Sophia Kerr, co-founding LuckyChap Entertainment, The Hollywood Reporter said in 2020.
“We were all like, ‘I want to make my own stuff,’” Robbie explained, while Ackerley added, “It was a bit of, ‘If (A-listers are) doing it, why can’t we?’”

Their first project, 2017’s “I, Tonya,” starred Robbie as ex-Olympic figure skater Tonya Harding. The film earned three Academy Award nominations (including one for Robbie) and one win for co-star Allison Janney.

“A lot of people cautioned us against starting a company with our friends, and I was actually really disappointed with how many people told us that it was a bad idea,” Robbie told Vogue Australia in 2018. “I guess we’re one of the exceptions to the rule because we’re still all the best of friends and we love working together. It’s perfect, because work never feels like work to me. I’m always with my best friends, I trust them implicitly, we know each other so well, we know everyone’s strengths and weaknesses and how to spread projects among ourselves so that we can be as productive and efficient as possible and it’s been great so far.”

Since then, LuckChap has created several films starring Robbie, including “Wuthering Heights” (2026), “Birds of Prey” (2020) and “Barbie” (2023). The company was also behind films like “Promising Young Woman,” which won best original screenplay at the 2021 Academy Awards and was nominated for best picture, best director, best actress and best film editing.
When Did Tom Ackerley and Margot Robbie Get Married?

In December 2016, the couple took many by surprise with a private wedding ceremony held in Byron Bay, Australia, reported Australia’s Daily Telegraph. Robbie was given away by her mom, and her three siblings — Anya, Lachlan and Cameron — also attended, according to the UK’s Daily Telegraph. But their engagement, if they’d officially had one, was never made public.

Robbie also showed off a photo of herself, featuring her smooching Ackerley with a raised ring finger adorned with a yellow-gold ring, reported Vogue in 2016. According to Ritani Diamonds, it’s a 1.5 carat pear-shaped diamond with a pavé diamond band.

As for the honeymoon, instead of traveling they made “I, Tonya,” as Robbie told “Extra” in 2017. “There were times we were sitting in a car parked in Atlanta freezing cold being like, ‘We should be on a beach right now,’” she said. “‘We should be on a honeymoon. What are we doing?!’ Following our dream.”
Post-“Tonya” they did travel to Tahiti, running into Ellen DeGeneres and Portia de Rossi, as well as former President Barack Obama while there.
As Robbie told DeGeneres in 2018 on “Ellen,” the newlyweds went to the hotel gym, with Ackerley wearing shorts that were a bit revealing. Naturally, they ran into the other three celebrities.

“We then had to do this whole like stretching class, but Tom meanwhile is trying to, like, not reveal anything, and he’s dying,” Robbie said. “The gym instructor keeps saying to Tom, ‘Tom, lunge! Lunge! You’re not lunging!’ and Tom’s trying to lunge cross-legged.”
Do Margot Robbie and Tom Ackerley Have Children?
Robbie and Ackerley have a son, who joined the family in the fall of 2024. The pair have not publicly shared their baby’s name or birthday, but have spoken about him in interviews.
After keeping the pregnancy under wraps, Robbie debuted her baby bump at events in the summer of 2024, including Wimbledon and while walking around Los Angeles.
When asked about keeping her child out of the spotlight, Robbie told British Vogue in December 2025 that she is “trying to keep that side of things private and protect him.”

“Earlier in my career, I’d speak more freely in interviews. I’ve just been burnt so many times, when people have taken what I’ve said out of context. And I read stuff all the time where people put me in quotation marks saying things I’ve never said.”
What Married Life is Like for Tom Ackerley and Margot Robbie
The pair will be celebrating a milestone 10-year wedding anniversary in 2026, now joined by their toddler.

Vogue Australia described a cozy scene at the couple’s Los Angeles home: “Ackerley walks into the kitchen just back from a workout and Robbie teases him affectionately about it being his second gym session of the day, before making him a cup of tea. The close bond between the pair, who married in an intimate ceremony in Byron Bay last December, is obvious. They briefly discuss scripts they have been reading and their plans for the evening — they are hosting a screening of ‘I, Tonya’ for the cast and crew of Dreamland. Ackerley then gives his wife a quick kiss and disappears into the vortex of the house …”
In the Dec. 2017 issue, in which Robbie served as the cover star, she told the magazine that her relationship with Ackerley has changed much since they first met.

“We were best friends and roommates before and now we’re like best friends and roommates still, so nothing’s really changed at all,” she said. “Other than the fact that I get to wear this on the weekends. I can’t obviously wear it during the week when I’m working — I don’t want to lose it on set.”

title: Carolyn Bessette Kennedy Didn't Want to Be Famous. That's Why Gen Z Loves Her
summary: Carolyn Bessette Kennedy became a Gen Z style icon according to a fashion expert and the woman who wrote a book about her
story: On social media, Carolyn Bessette Kennedy isn’t just a style icon, she’s an entire aesthetic.

Type her name into TikTok and you’ll find thousands of videos: “CBK” gift guides, capsule wardrobes inspired by her minimalist looks, creators showing “what Carolyn would wear to a bar,” even pilgrimages to C.O. Bigelow, the New York apothecary where she reportedly frequented.
Nearly 30 years after her death in 1999, she has become Gen Z’s patron saint of quiet luxury.

Bessette left almost nothing behind. No social media footprint. Few interviews. No carefully curated public persona. Just fleeting paparazzi photos of a woman who, by most accounts, desperately wanted to live a private life. That scarcity has become part of the allure.

“What’s most remarkable about her style is the fact that none of her outfits look outdated over 25 years later. Not one,” says Erin Fitzpatrick, a veteran fashion editor and associate director of fashion news at Who What Wear, whose TikTok account breaks down fashion and style across pop culture, with over ten videos devoted solely to Bessette's looks.
“That’s impressive and shows how laser-focused she was on collecting truly classic pieces.”

On TikTok, creators dissect every element of her look: the neutrals, the streamlined tailoring, the mixing of brown and black, the near-total absence of logos or jewelry— even at her 1996 wedding, where she wore a bias-cut silk gown designed by her close friend Narciso Rodriguez. They note how often she re-wore the same staples: corduroy pants, medium-wash jeans, long-sleeved tops with the sleeves pushed up just so. The uniform was simple but deliberate:  high-quality clothes, impeccable grooming, restraint.
That restraint, however, may be what resonates most now.

Elizabeth Beller, author of “Once Upon A Time: The Captivating Life of Carolyn Bessette Kennedy,” the book that inspired the new FX on Hulu series, believes Gen Z’s fascination isn’t just about minimalism— it’s about what Bessette refused to become.
“It was very clear she wasn’t interested in fame. She was interested in John for who he was,” Beller tells TODAY.com.
In an era when, as she notes, nearly every moment of life is documented and shared, that resistance feels almost radical.

For a generation raised on Instagram grids, personal brands and constant self-documentation, Bessette represents a different value system. According to Beller, she was uninterested in celebrity for celebrity’s sake — a sharp contrast to an era where people feel obligated to post their life online to create a platform.

At the time, the reluctance was often weaponized against her. When Bessette declined to “participate” in the paparazzi spectacle, Beller says, a narrative was constructed in her absence, and it wasn’t always kind. Decades later, that same refusal reads less like aloofness and more like integrity.

Still, Bessette's influence extends beyond nostalgia. Fitzpatrick sees her imprint across modern brands, like Khaite and The Row, which prize clean lines and understated luxury. The “quiet luxury” aesthetic that has dominated fashion discourse in recent years feels like a direct descendant of her wardrobe.

Yet reducing Bessette to an aesthetic risks flattening her into something she likely would have resisted. Before she became a tabloid fixture, she built a serious career in public relations at Calvin Klein. But after marrying into one of America’s most famous families, paparazzi attention made it nearly impossible to continue working.
She left her job in 1996. She would live only three more years.

Beller argues that Bessette's staying power lies in more than impeccable tailoring. Friends described her as warm, loyal and deeply funny, qualities that rarely made it into headlines or translate to photos. The public fixation on her image obscured a fuller picture of who she was.

But perhaps that’s part of the appeal, too. In a culture saturated with access, overexposure and personal branding, Carolyn Bessette Kennedy feels unknowable. She wasn’t performing for an audience. She wasn’t optimizing herself for clicks. She simply dressed well, lived as privately as she could, and refused to turn herself into content.
Now, decades later, the internet has done that for her.

The slip dresses and minimalist wardrobes will continue to circulate. The wedding dress will remain pinned to vision boards. But beneath the aesthetic is something quieter: a woman who tried, however imperfectly, to draw a boundary around her own life.
In an age built on visibility, that kind of restraint feels timeless.

title: Everything Figure Skater Alysa Liu Has Said About Her ‘Alternative’ Style
summary: After capturing gold in the women’s free skate at the 2026 Winter Olympics, Alysa Liu has fans asking: What is on her teeth? Here’s what to know about her frenulum piercing.
story: Alysa Liu's skating skills have been on full display during the 2026 Winter Olympics, but the American athlete has also caught viewers' attention for her bold sense of style.
The 20-year-old has been crushing it at the 2026 Winter Olympics and is currently in third place following her short program on Feb. 17. She will take part in the free skate competition on Feb. 19.
On the ice, Liu always looks put together in dazzling costumes, but she also lets a touch of sass shine through.
Below, we're sharing everything she's said about her personal sense of style.
She Has a Frenulum Piercing, or a 'Smiley'
Whenever Liu flashes a smile, it's hard not to notice her frenulum piercing, or what she calls her "smiley."

Per the Cleveland Clinic, a frenulum, also known as a frenum, is a "thin, ropelike piece of tissue that connects two bodily structures." In your mouth, you have two types of frenulums: a tongue and a lip frenulum. The lip frenulum is a piece of tissue that connects your lips to your gums.
In an interview with NBC, Liu said she pierced her lip frenulum two years ago.
"I did it myself. I had my sister hold up my lip and I was like looking in the mirror and I had my piercing needle and then, yeah, I just put it through," she said.
There's a Special Story Behind Her Hairstyle

Liu's multicolored hair has many viewers doing a double take. At the top of her head, the look starts with a layer of brown then transitions to blond and repeats until it reaches the ends of her hair, which are brown.
While speaking with NBC, Liu explained what inspired the unique hairstyle.
“You know how trees have rings for their age? I thought, every year I’m gonna add a new halo around my hair,” she said.

In a separate interview with NBC Bay Area, Liu said she has been rocking the hairstyle for three years, since 2023. At first, she attempted to create the look herself, but soon consulted professional help.

"I wanted it to be lighter because usually I do it like a gingery color, but I was like, 'I want something different. Like, I want it lighter.' And so, I made it lighter. And I realized like the second round of bleach, it’s really hard to get it lighter because my hair is so dark, and so you need like salon-grade products. So I went to a salon in St. Louis," she recalled.
Liu's stylist then took over and got her hair to a healthy place.
"This is exactly what I wanted," she said.
She Loves Fashion
In an interview with Cosmopolitan, Liu explained why she loves masculine style “so much.”

“I feel really comfortable in it. Off the ice, I don’t dress as feminine. To me, that is skating. I’ll wear skirts, too, but I definitely love a good masculine street style. When I am at public events and I’m wearing my usual style, people will say, ‘Oh, I thought you were, like, a snowboarder.’ I take it as a compliment. I like throwing people off,” she said.
Liu is a fashionista at heart, and she recently told Elle that she designs her own dresses.
"I’m so into fashion, and I love being able to control little things. Even my training outfits, I get to choose now. Before, I didn’t. Isn’t that crazy? But now I do," she said.
While speaking with NBC, Liu said she adores fashion.
"You know, someone called my style alternative and I’d agree with that," she said.
She Has Creative Control Over Her Look on the Ice
In her interview with Cosmopolitan, Liu said she made a deal with her ice skating coaches so that she could express herself creatively amid her comeback.
Liu, who competed at the Beijing Winter Olympics, announced her retirement from skating in 2022 at age 16.
When she returned to the sport, she did it on her terms.
"No one tells me what I’m gonna wear. No one tells me how my hair is gonna be. No one’s gonna try to change me," she said.
Liu's coaches did warn her that some judges and "higher-ups" might "be concerned" with her look, but it didn't faze her.

"I said if they tell me to dye my hair back, I will quit. If they don’t like it and they want to give me less scores or treat me differently, that’s on them. If I change my hair, it’s gonna be because I wanted to," she said.

title: People Are Re-Reading 'Wuthering Heights.' It Isn't Going Well
summary: People are rereading Wuthering Heights for the new movie. An English professor breaks down why Wuthering Heights might be hard to read.
story: Emerald Fennell’s “Wuthering Heights” adaptation has some readers reaching for the original Emily Brontë novel for a re-read. Only this time, they’re struggling to get through it.
“I am re-reading ‘Wuthering Heights’ before the movie comes out, and it has not taken me long to realize that there is some brain rot happening,” influencer Mary Skinner said in a TikTok post.

“This is not the brain that got the English degree,” she continued. “This has honestly been a wakeup call for me, I don’t think I’ve read anything other than books that have been easily digestible in over six months. I’m finding this to be much more challenging than I would have a couple years ago.”
Skinner is far from the only one adjusting to the classics. “Need a brain break after 3 pages,” TikToker Athina Martin wrote in the caption of her post.
“I’m trying so hard to read ‘Wuthering Heights’ before the movie comes out but I’m struggling,” another video's caption reads.
In the face of these challenges, one TikToker chose to read a version of the book re-written in modern English. “I need to understand what I’m reading,” she said.
"Wuthering Heights" was first published in 1847 under the pen name Ellis Bell, a name which Brontë selected to conceal her identity.

In the book, a young woman named Catherine Earnshaw and Heathcliff, an orphan Catherine's father brings back to Yorkshire, develop an obsessive bond. The novel shocked 19th century audiences upon its release and later became more widely acclaimed.

Rosemary Haskell, a professor of English at Elon University who specializes in British literature, said the book’s appeal comes down to its daring content. The book, Haskell says, is “full of cruelty, violence and bloodshed,” juxtaposed with “passionate and undying love.”
“It appeals to our slightly shameful and subversive desires to experience the terror and horror ourselves and to experience other people undergoing terror and horror,” Haskell says.
Today, it’s a staple staple on high school curricula and a favorite for screen adaptations (over 30 so far).
In an interview with TODAY.com, Skinner says she feels a marked difference between this “Wuthering Heights” attempt and the first time she read it.
“I realized after starting the book that I had lost some of my attention span and reading comprehension over the years,” Skinner says.
Skinner suspects that her new routine of “doom scrolling” and reading easier novels might make it tougher to switch to a book that requires more concentration.

“I used to easily read classics, but a few years of a largely unvaried reading diet consisting of mainly modern mysteries, romances, fantasies, and quick reads, plus years of chasing quick dopamine by consuming short form content online, was making it harder to have the patience and attention to read ‘Wuthering Heights,’” she says.

Though the book’s challenges may also be inherent. Haskell said the perceived difficulty of reading grade school classics like “Wuthering Heights” can also be attributed to a changing English language, not just doomscrolling.

“I think the way that people write and the way that people talk shifts over time,” she says. Other potential challenges include “the terminology, the sentence structure and the density of the prose,” plus the “incomprehensible Yorkshire dialect.”

Virginia Rademacher, a professor of literary and cultural studies and the Chair of the Arts and Humanities Division at Babson College, also says the difficulty comes down to the narrator. The book has multiple narrators and also unfolds in letters.

“It's told by these unreliable narrators. We don’t actually know of what we’re being told about (the characters) is completely true either, because we can’t trust the various people that are telling the story," Rademacher says.
Now, BookTok is full of videos coaching people through their first “Wuthering Heights” read or re-read.

One recommended looking up words and plot lines while reading. Another Booktoker said to read the book in an accent: “I am not kidding you.” Another created a video highlighting what readers should pay attention to early on in the book, giving a hint at what would later be important.
Haskell’s advice for anyone looking to start a classic? Read the book out loud and pair the reading experience with watching adaptations, which can make the text more accessible.
“In the end, I think you just have to persevere and keep going. It becomes easier," Haskell says.

title: ‘Love Is Blind’ Fans Call Out Season 10 Star Chris for Comments About Jessica’s Fitness
summary: After one couple from “Love Is Blind” Season 10 decided to split, viewers are calling out a contestant for how he handled the breakup.
story: Warning: Spoilers from “Love Is Blind” Season 10 below…
After one couple from “Love Is Blind” Season 10 decided to split, viewers are calling out a contestant for how he handled the breakup.

In the first group of episodes from the Ohio-based season, which premiered Feb. 11, fans watched contestants Chris Fusco and Jessica Barrett fall in love in the pods and become one of seven couples to get engaged. But in the most recent episodes, out Feb. 18, Fusco revealed he was struggling to develop a physical connection with his fiancée.

Episode 8 ended with Barrett returning to her shared apartment with Fusco to discuss the sudden tension between them. During the conversation, Fusco said that outside of the experiment he was used to dating women who do CrossFit.

“Just somebody who does f—ing Pilates every day, or someone who’s working out every day,” he added while explaining his usual type.  He also shared that he had spent the past two days thinking about his other connection in the pods with contestant Bri McNees.
Episode 9 began with Barrett processing Fusco’s feelings and comments.
“I mean, all of these things are really hard to hear,” she told him.
Barrett continued, “Honestly, the fact that me not working out every day is even a sentence that came up in this conversation is blowing my mind.”
Barrett, an infectious diseases physician, pointed out that she spends the majority of her day caring for patients.

“I was very upfront about that from the beginning. There was no illusion that I was going to be going to Pilates every day,” she said. “It makes me feel like s—, too. Like, not good enough. In a way that I can’t control. Very inadequate.”
The conversation ended with Barrett leaving after telling Fusco, “If my body isn’t good enough for you, I’m never going to be, like, ‘Oh, please still love me.’ That’s not what I’m here for.”
The two were not shown talking again and viewers later learned in the episode that they had officially ended their engagement.
Fans voiced their thoughts about the fallout between Fusco and Barrett on X, with many writing that his comments were unexpected.
“Ew Chris did a complete 180 since Cabo what is this,” one asked.
Another baffled viewer wrote, “She’s a doctor and she’s fit so I’m confused on what Chris is sayin.”

A third said, “Im sorry did chris expect jessica to just keep smiling and stay with him after he told her to her face that he doesnt think shes physically attractive and is thinking about his other choice???”
Others said they didn’t understand why Fusco chose to apply for “Love Is Blind.”
“How does Chris go on a show like love is blind and then make complaints about Jessica’s body. Don’t come on the show if you have strict physical preferences,” one tweet said.
One fan simply described Fusco as “diabolical.”
Fusco addressed his comments in an interview with Entertainment Weekly.
“I mean, looking back, I definitely would’ve expressed the words that came out of my mouth differently,” he said. “I really just wanted to be completely honest with her about how I was feeling.”
Fusco said he respects Barrett and called her “an amazing person.”

He added, “I felt like maybe just being super direct and honest about how I felt was the best route at the time, and just explaining what I’m normally attracted to physically without... because I mean, on the show, we’re meeting someone based on emotional connection, so we’re doing it pretty much backwards than the normal dating life.”
Fusco said he didn’t intend to hurt Barrett, but he understands why she was upset.

Although his engagement is over, it seems Fusco will still be featured in future episodes as a teaser showed him chatting with McNees about his unresolved feelings for her. Fans will see how she reacts to his remarks when the next batch of episodes are released Feb. 25.

title: Megan Thee Stallion Won’t Reveal What’s In Her Signature Baked Beans, so I Tried to Figure it Out
summary: Megan Thee Stallion wouldn't reveal what is in her family's famous barbecue baked bean dish, so I did some research and tried re-creating the recipe.
story: Megan Thee Stallion, much like her native Texas, is rich, complex and closely associated with thoroughbreds. These words can also be used describe her signature baked beans.
Last month, I interviewed the three-time Grammy winner, who spoke about her catchy jingle for Dunkin’ and what she had cooking on stage and at home.
Megan regularly flexes her culinary muscles online, sharing restaurant reviews and cooking tutorials, so I tried to get the skinny on a particular side dish she showed her fans on Thanksgiving.

That day, she met boyfriend Klay Thompson’s family, and she made several dishes for the event: dressing, mac and cheese and turkey, but I was drawn to a saucy bean dish on her table. They had an ostentatious flair about them. I had to know what was in them.
She wouldn’t tell me, though.

“I mean, I can’t just be giving out my secret recipes now. My grandma would be in an uproar, OK?” she told me at the time, but she did offer a dash of knowledge I could use: “Just know that it’s packed with lots of meat, lots of vegetables.”
I had forgotten about the dish until she posted about it again on Jan. 31, making it for Thompson’s dad who was at the couple’s house for a visit.
It then became my mission to make those beans.
Time for Recipe Testing

Dissecting her video from by frame, I spotted red pepper, green pepper, onions, ground beef and sausage in her saute pan. Doing a bit of research based on these ingredients, I found this dish has a name  Texans sometimes use (and other times don’t): cowboy beans.

Cowboy beans are a Southwestern dish created during the cattle drives of the 19th century, using what they had on hand — dried pinto beans, bacon, onions and spices — to stew in cast-iron pots and make a filling meal. Can’t you just hear the harmonica playing “Home on the Range”?

“Hogar en la Pradera” may be more appropriate, though, since those stateside cowboys got the idea for the dish from Mexican cowboys, or vaqueros, who made their original, more soup-like version, frijoles charros, with much less meat than those early Texans.
But, I thought, Megan’s dish must be distinct in some way from cowboy beans. I mean, she’s Thee Stallion — there must be a secret ingredient.
Auditioning Secret Ingredients
Before I scoured the condiment aisle of my local supermarket, I asked my good friend, writer and cookbook author Allison Robicelli, how she’s tested theories for recipes in the past.
“Guesses for possible secret ingredients: liquid smoke, pancake syrup, French onion soup mix,” Robicelli texted me, adding that as long as I have a plan of action, I’m golden.
In modern cowboy bean recipes, barbecue sauce, mustard, ketchup, garlic, brown sugar and other ingredients are combined with cans of baked beans and meats like ground beef and sausage.

After parsing through a few recipes — one from YouTuber Emmymade, another extremely viral one from chef Darius and ones from various recipe blogs — I did my first test, which was really four-in-one: I made four batches of beans and, at the end, added the three secret ingredients that Robicelli suggested, plus molasses in the fourth, which I though would make the dish sweet and mysteriously deep.

After chucking the maple syrup version — which tasted like a beany dessert — I tried the French onion version, which was too salty but salvageable for lunch on a lazy day. Liquid smoke was a good call by Robicelli and Darius, and the molasses did exactly what it needed to do.

For my final batch, I went with andouille sausage for a kick and oregano for some herbiness inspired by frijoles charros. And because this dish has a whatever-you-have-lying-around vibe, I also added a full package of bacon, as well as Dijon for a touch of sophistication.
It tasted like “Savage” sounds, and that’s all I need to say about it.
Conclusions

Since I used sight and context clues, as well as a bit of Texas history, I could be close to he recipe, but I’m probably not right about it, and that’s OK with me. It was a fun journey, and now I can just about rap “Bigger in Texas” from memory after playing it on repeat as I worked in the kitchen.
“Ain’t nobody seeing me on barbecue baked beans,” Megan said in the video that set me on my mission.

That may be true, but I’ll be damned if I don’t have a fridge full of beany, meaty goodness that transports me to the balmy open range while I sit in the middle of an East Coast cold snap. So, as Megan says, I’m at the top of my game.
Megan Thee Stallion-Style Cowboy Beans Recipe
Prepare yourself to be the life of the party with a hefty portion of yummy goodness, because this makes a lot of beans.
You can halve this recipe by only using one can of baked beans. You can also make it your own with herbs, meats or whatever you got in the fridge.
1 (13-ounce) package andouille sausages, cut into little pieces or rounds1 pound ground beef1 (12-ounce) package bacon, diced
1 (13-ounce) package andouille sausages, cut into little pieces or rounds
1 pound ground beef
1 (12-ounce) package bacon, diced
1 white onion, diced
1 white onion, diced

5 bell peppers, diced (you can use green, red or a combo of colors like Meg does)4-5 cloves fresh garlic, roughly chopped1 bunch herbs like oregano, tarragon or parsley, torn2 (28-ounce) cans baked beans2 tablespoons Dijon mustard (yellow is also fine)3 tablespoons barbecue sauce1 teaspoon black pepper2 tablespoons molasses8 drops liquid smoke1/3 cup brown sugar
5 bell peppers, diced (you can use green, red or a combo of colors like Meg does)
4-5 cloves fresh garlic, roughly chopped
1 bunch herbs like oregano, tarragon or parsley, torn
2 (28-ounce) cans baked beans
2 tablespoons Dijon mustard (yellow is also fine)
3 tablespoons barbecue sauce
1 teaspoon black pepper
2 tablespoons molasses
8 drops liquid smoke
1/3 cup brown sugar
Preheat your oven to 400 F.
Take out a deep casserole dish or other large pot for the oven. I used a 12- by 17-inch disposable roaster pan, which you can get at most supermarkets.
In a large saute pan or cast-iron skillet over medium-high heat, brown the sausage. Once browned, place  it on a paper towel-lined plate.

Place the whole package of ground beef into the same pan (with all that precious fond), break it apart with a wooden spoon and cook until browned. If there is a lot of liquid at this point, drain it. Set the beef aside on another paper towel-lined plate.
Place the bacon in the pan, cook it until its browned and crispy, and set it aside on yet another paper towel-lined plate.
Add the onion and peppers to the pan full of bacon drippings. Sweat the veggies for 5 to 6 minutes, until soft. Add the minced garlic and whatever herbs you have. Stir to combine.
Remove pan from the heat and get ready to assemble your dish.
Add the baked beans with sauce to your roaster pan, along with the sausage, ground beef, bacon, vegetable mix and the remaining ingredients. Stir to combine everything.
Place in the oven for 45 minutes. Once out of the oven, stir, season to taste and serve hot over rice or just on its own.

title: Where the 'America's Next Top Model' Judges' Relationships with Tyra Banks Stand Now
summary: Where are the America's Next Top Model judges now? Find out what happened to Jay Manuel, Miss J, Nigel Barker and where their relationships with Tyra stand.
story: “America’s Next Top Model” was a cultural phenomenon when it debuted in 2003.
The modeling competition show was created and hosted by Tyra Banks, with Jay Manuel serving as creative director and J. Alexander, AKA Miss Jay, as the show’s runway coach.

“ANTM” ran for 24 seasons from 2003 to 2018, with various judges and fashion experts joining the panel and giving their honest (and at types cruel) opinions of the contestants. Over the years, as people started rewatching or watching the show for the first time on streaming platforms, it came under criticism and received backlash for how the contestants were treated and harshly critiqued over their weight and looks.

Among the controversial resurfaced clips include Banks having cycle 6 winner Danielle Evans to get the gap in her teeth fixed, despite her reluctance. Banks would later apologize to Evans for the action. There’s also the infamous moment where the supermodel yelled at cycle 4 contestant Tiffany Richardson andshouted, “We were all rooting for you!”
Now, a new Netflix documentary, “Reality Check: Inside America’s Next Top Model,” is pulling back the curtain and showing “the chaos in front and behind the camera.”

What started a “glamorous launchpad for aspiring models,” as Netflix describes, “became a pop-culture juggernaut defined by explosive drama, public meltdowns and controversies that still fuel viral moments today.”

The three-part documentary series includes Banks herself speaking out about the backlash, as well as her journey to getting the show made, the ratings highs, and the lows, which included being kicked off her own show.
It also checks in with Jay Manuel, Miss Jay and photographer Nigel Barker, who all share their experience being on “America’s Next Top Model.”
See where the original “America’s Next Top Model” team, including prominent judges, are now.
Tyra Banks
Tyra Banks created “America’s Next Top Model” in hopes of showing the behind the scenes of the modeling world and giving diverse models a chance at entering the business.
Banks would ultimately be replaced as a host for Season 23, with Rita Ora replacing her. Banks did come back for the final Season 24.
The supermodel continues to be an entrepreneur, most recently creating an Australian ice cream brand, Smize and Dream, a “hot ice cream” for all to enjoy.

She also launched at the beginning of this year, SMiZE Ice Cream Magazine, an Instagram carousel magazine. She continues to model and even release music here and there, including her 2025 holiday track, “Santa SMiZE, Santa SMiZE.”
And while Banks said in “Reality Check” that her work in “not done” on “America’s Next Top Model” and hopes for another season, no confirmation about the renewal of the show has been made.
Jay Manuel
Jay Manuel was Banks’ makeup artist and was tapped to be the show’s creative director. He would also go on to become a judge on the panel.

The two were very close and had a strong friendship. However, as “Reality Check” shows, their relationship hit a pause after Manuel wanted to leave the show after Season 17. In the docuseries, he expressed how the show’s direction was changing and he wanted to pursue other projects.
He said that he sent Banks an email thanking her for the opportunity but wanted leave.
"She didn’t respond. It was probable the longest three days ever,” Manuel said in the documentary. “And she ultimately wrote back just three words: I am disappointed.”
He said that “all communication stopped between” him and Banks.
When asked to comment on their friendship, Banks said, “Nah. I’d prefer... I... Yeah, I should call Jay. I don’t want to do this here. But he’s a special man.”
Manuel did one more season before he, Miss Jay and Nigel Barker were fired.
Following the show, Manuel worked with E!, covering red carpets. He also launched a cosmetics company “Jay Manuel Beauty Retail Experience,” and wrote a book, “The Wig, the Bitch and the Meltdown.”
Miss Jay
Jay Alexander, also known as Miss Jay, was the show's runway coach and judge. He taught the models, including Banks, how to perfect their runway walk.
He and Banks knew each other since she was 16 and revered by the fashion industry. He was on the show for 18 seasons before being let go. He returned as a judge for Season 21 and 22.
Miss Jay would go on to work on other “America’s Next Top Model” versions around the world. In “Reality Check,” he reveals that on Dec. 27, 2022, he suffered a stroke.
He spent five weeks in a coma and couldn't walk or talk.

“I miss being the queen of the runway, the catwalks, of course. I’m the person who taught models how to walk. I taught models how to walk and now I can’t walk. Not yet. Not yet. I’m determined to walk. I’m sure you’re going to see me again. I’m sure it’s not over for me yet,” he said.
While in the hospital, Manuel and photographer Nigel Barker visited him, while Banks did not.
Nigel Barker
Another popular figure in “America’s Next Top Model“ was photographer Nigel Barker. The creative joined the show in Season 2 as a judge and photographer.

In “Reality Check” Barker shares how he joined the team as the “straight man eye candy.” He was a part of some of the most iconic and controversial shoots until Season 18, when he was also fired. He returned as a guest for the show's final season.

After "ANTM," Barker worked with Taylor Swift, the Miss Universe  and Miss USA competitions. He also released a couple of fashion and photography books, including "Models of Influence: 50 Women Who Reset the Course of Fashion."
He is also the co-founder of The Barker Company, an alcohol brand that created it's own espresso martini.
Janice Dickinson

Supermodel Janice Dickinson was an original judge on the show from Season 1 to Season 4. For Season 5 to 7, she returned as a guest and recurring judge. Kimora Lee Simmons and Beau Quillian were also original judges but only for Season 1.
Dickinson was known for her harsh critiques of the contestants and butting heads with Banks over their opinions about the models.
While Dickinson is not featured in “Reality Check,” she does appear in another upcoming E! docuseries, “Dirty Rotten Scandals.”

After “ANTM,” she would go on to appear in other reality shows like “Celebrity Big Brother,” “House of Villains,” “The Dark Side of Reality TV” and “I’m a Celebrity Get Me Out of Here,” among other shows. These days, the 80s supermodel critiques people's runway walks on her Instagram.

title: Read the Beginning of 'Laws of Love and Logic,' a New Book From Jenna's Imprint
summary: Debra Curtis says one question "absolutely consumes" Lily Webb, the protagonist of her debut novel, "Laws of Love and Logic."What if
story: Debra Curtis says one question "absolutely consumes" Lily Webb, the protagonist of her debut novel, "Laws of Love and Logic."
What if?
The book, out from Jenna Bush Hager's Thousand Voices x Random House Publishing Group imprint, is an "epic love story about a woman torn between her first love and her husband," Jenna said on TODAY.
Curtis, 62, is making her fiction debut. She is a retired professor of cultural anthropology at Salve Regina University, where she specialized in gender and sexuality.
Speaking to TODAY.com, Curtis said the book "feels like a long conversation I’ve been having with myself for 30 years."

While reading, Curtis said she hopes people ask themselves: "Could I ever truly forgive the unforgivable? If all life is interconnected, does it require us to love differently?And for female readers especially: how am I complicit, even unintentionally, in the subordination of other women?"

"Laws of Love and Logic" took Curtis 10 years to write, and the impact of this moment is not lost on her. "Given that I received well over a hundred rejections from literary agents, it still feels a bit unbelievable," she said.
Below, read the opening of the book.
Read the Beginning of 'Laws of Love and Logic'

Early fall. Leaves of gold on the birch shimmered when the wind picked up. When no one answered the front door, Lily walked around the side of the house to the back deck. She could hear her boyfriend’s father playing guitar and singing softly, a melancholy tune—­ familiar, but she couldn’t quite make it out. The boy, like his dad, was musically inclined. Playing the guitar had a calming effect on Mr. Cooper, especially after his son’s football games.

The back deck was unfinished, littered with tools and cans of paint with rusted lids—­ neglected since last spring—­ alongside a spare tire, two fishing rods, and a cooler. The boy’s dad was on his seventh beer; by the time the day was over he would have finished off a case by himself. Lily didn’t want to disturb Mr. Cooper and waited until he looked up from his guitar.
“There’s my favorite girl,” he said with a big smile, resting his forearm on the body of the instrument.
“Don’t stop on account of me,” Lily said. “I love hearing you play.”
Mr. Cooper, still smiling, shook his head and leaned his guitar against a post on the deck.
“Some game, huh?” he said as he stood up.
He offered Lily his seat, the only one on the deck: a mustard-­ and-orange-­webbed aluminum lawn chair. When she refused to sit, he remained standing.

It had been an exciting game that morning: Portsmouth against Barrington, rival teams. The boy, a senior at Portsmouth and the team’s quarterback, had read the defenses instantly, successfully changing the play at the line of scrimmage three times when Barrington’s defense had anticipated Portsmouth’s plays and was prepared to stop them.

When the boy appeared at the screen door in a pair of jeans and a Creedence Clearwater Revival T-­ shirt, toweling off his long brown hair, his dad couldn’t contain himself and gave the boy a hug, patting his back.
“Four hundred and ninety passing yards! That’s a record. One game! That’s my boy!” Mr. Cooper was smiling from ear to ear, so excited that he spat as he talked.
The boy watched Lily. She never seemed to mind that his father always drank and talked endlessly about the boy’s football stats.
“Everybody knows he’s the best the state has ever seen. When he was fourteen and there were sixty seconds left on the clock, he threw the ball fifty yards and that Portuguese kid caught it.”
“Lil knows the story,” the boy said, putting his arm around his dad. He was tall like his father but a whole lot bigger.
“I was there, Mr. Cooper—­ I saw the whole thing. Just like Roger Staubach!”
“Marry this girl!” the boy’s father said. He took a long swig of his beer.

The boy knew that over the past five decades, the little state of Rhode Island had produced three professional quarterbacks; he was determined to be the fourth. He was ten years old when Joe Namath predicted that the Jets would beat the Colts in the 1969 Super Bowl.
As a young kid, the boy had borrowed Namath’s courage and bold ness. Sometimes, standing in the locker room in front of his team mates, he would say, “We’re going to win this one, I guarantee it.”

The boy took Lily’s hand, led her inside, and pulled the screen door shut behind him. He took two beers out of the refrigerator, and as he and Lily made their way downstairs to the basement, they heard his father: “Chuck at the hardware store said that the scouts will be coming around to have a look at you!”

When the boy was six, his mom took off and moved to Florida with the golf pro from Green Valley golf course. For Christmases and birthdays, she sent her son cards. The boy had learned that his mother was playing piano in a bar in Boca Raton. Back then no one had ever heard of Boca Raton but the boy found it on a map when he was in the second grade. Folks in town heard that the golf pro hit her occasionally and got one of the waitresses pregnant at the resort where he worked.

Every trophy the boy had ever won was displayed on the wooden shelves his father made for him. While football was his main sport, he also played basketball and baseball. The largest trophy was the one he’d received the prior year for football: MVP, a pewter sculpted figure —­ poised to throw a football —­ mounted on a wooden base. Free weights and a bench filled one side of the boy’s basement room, with a bed, a TV, a set of drawers, and an old electric keyboard on the other side. In the corner was a brand-­ new Sony stereo, which his father had recently bought him for his eighteenth birthday. Albums were care fully lined up between two cinder blocks.
Lily placed her beer near a stack of library books on the chest of drawers and picked up the boy’s sunglasses. Putting them on, she stretched out on his bed. The boy sat down at the keyboard.
“Did I ever tell you that my mom played the organ at Saint Barnabas and that’s where she met my dad?”
Lily turned her head on the pillow to watch him. He had told her. this, but she didn’t mind hearing it again, not in the least. “Tell me” was all she said.
“He fell in love with her on a Sunday in between the Gloria and Communion.”
“That’s beautiful.”

“After my mom left, he just couldn’t go back there, so that’s why we go to Saint Anthony’s. I can remember our last Christmas together. Although, I didn’t know it would be our last. She played ‘O Holy Night’ at Mass. I was real sad watching my dad cry.”
Lily swung her legs over the side of the bed and sat up. Before she could say anything, he changed the subject and said, “Do you know this song?”
His hands moved tentatively across the black and white keys.

Like birds pecking in the sand, his fingers bent lightly. He started and stopped a couple of times, but when he was able to pull it together, combining the chords with the right individual notes, Lily exclaimed,
“How did you learn that?”
A little soft, but melodic, the boy sang: “The screen door slams,
Mary’s dress sways / Like a vision she dances . . . ”

As the boy worked out the chords to “Thunder Road,” Lily slipped her hand under his shirt and caressed his back. Born to Run was released the year before, and Lily and the boy both unknowingly bought the other the album for Christmas. The boy stopped playing and looked up at Lily. “That’s as far as I’ve gotten.”
“Call in sick tonight,” Lily said.
“I can’t. You know that.”
“What’s a party without the team quarterback?”
“Are you still planning on going?”

When Lily didn’t answer, the boy rose from the keyboard bench and moved over to his bed. He worked Saturday and Sunday nights, but never Fridays, not the night before a game. He washed dishes at Reidy’s diner on East Main Road. Late into the shift, when it was just the owner and the boy, the owner would fill Styrofoam containers with mashed potatoes, green beans, and meat loaf. The Saturday night special. He’d always add a couple of desserts, one for the boy and one for his father.
The boy sat on the edge of his bed. Lily followed him. She got down on her knees and wiggled herself in between his thighs.
“I promise you’ll have a good time,” Lily said.

She and the boy had been together since they were freshmen, and while they had yet to have sex, they delighted in each other’s caresses with hands and mouths, here in the basement or in the field behind the school. With the tip of her finger, Lily moved a strand of her hair away from her face.
“I don’t understand why you want to go to the party without me,” the boy said.
“Am I supposed to sit home every Saturday night my senior year because you have to work?”
The boy looked away.
“I’m sorry,” she added.

And she was. She knew the boy helped his father out with the basic bills: electricity, groceries, and sometimes the oil bill when the winters were at their worst. The boy had his first job when he was thirteen, a paper route. The factory in Fall River, where his father had worked for fifteen years and planned on working for the rest of his life, had closed down.
